title: The Crusader Kingdoms
subtitle: Rise and Fall of Outremer, 1099–1291 AD
tagline: For two hundred years, European Crusaders held Jerusalem and built kingdoms in the heart of the Islamic world. This is how they rose, how they ruled, and why they could never last.
villain_mandate: Saladin must be portrayed as genuinely, historically great — a chivalric and brilliant opponent. The Crusader defeat at Hattin must feel genuinely decisive and devastating. The fall of Jerusalem must feel like the inevitable conclusion of structural failure, not just military loss.

scenes.title: Jerusalem Falls — Then Is Taken
scenes.narration: 1099. The First Crusade had marched from France to the Holy Land — three years, four thousand miles, half the army dead of starvation, disease, and battle. When they reached Jerusalem, they besieged it for five weeks. On July 15, 1099, they breached the walls. The city fell. The Crusaders held Jerusalem for eighty-eight years. They built an entire civilization there. Then they lost it. Then they spent two hundred years trying to get it back.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Jerusalem at sunset — the holy city, the Dome of the Rock, the Church of the Holy Sepulchre. The First Crusade arriving after three years and four thousand miles: exhausted, reduced, but reaching their destination. The siege. The breach. Then: Crusader kingdoms established — castles, churches, markets. A civilization in the desert. Then: the beginning of the end. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Crusader States
scenes.narration: Outremer — 'beyond the sea' in French — was what they called the Crusader kingdoms. Four states along the Levantine coast: the Kingdom of Jerusalem, the County of Tripoli, the Principality of Antioch, the County of Edessa. The Crusaders built three hundred castles, traded with local populations, married into local families, developed their own culture that was neither fully European nor fully Levantine. For eighty-eight years, they held the holiest city in Christendom.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. The Crusader states shown on a map — four kingdoms along the Levantine coast, surrounded by Islamic powers on all sides. The Crusader castles: Krak des Chevaliers, Belvoir, Kerak — magnificent fortifications. Crusader knights and local populations coexisting in markets and courts. A civilization that shouldn't have existed and lasted almost two centuries. 16:9.

scenes.title: Saladin
scenes.narration: Salah ad-Din Yusuf ibn Ayyub — Saladin — was a Kurdish Muslim general who unified Egypt and Syria under his rule through a combination of military genius, political skill, and personal reputation for chivalry that his Crusader enemies acknowledged. He declared jihad to retake Jerusalem. He was patient, methodical, and brilliant. He was also generous to his enemies in a way the Crusaders had not been to Muslims in 1099. This made him more dangerous — not less.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Saladin — a real historical figure: Kurdish general, middle-aged, commanding presence, the expression of someone who thinks three moves ahead. Shown unifying his forces, receiving ambassadors, treating captured Crusader knights with notable courtesy. His reputation preceding him into battles before they're fought. The Crusader kings taking him seriously in a way they didn't take other opponents. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Horns of Hattin
scenes.narration: July 4, 1187. Saladin lured the Crusader army away from water on the hottest day of the year, across dry ground, toward the twin peaks called the Horns of Hattin. He set the grass on fire. The Crusader army — fifteen thousand men in full armor, in July heat, with no water, surrounded by smoke — fell apart. The True Cross was captured. The King of Jerusalem was captured. The greatest Crusader army ever assembled was destroyed in one afternoon.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. The Horns of Hattin — twin volcanic peaks in the Galilean hills. The Crusader army marching in the July heat toward the water they can't reach. Saladin's forces surrounding them. The grass fires starting. Smoke. Armored knights in the July sun with no water, surrounded. The Crusader formation collapsing. The True Cross falling. The King of Jerusalem taken prisoner. 16:9.

scenes.title: Jerusalem, Again
scenes.narration: October 2, 1187. Saladin entered Jerusalem. He had negotiated the surrender — paid ransoms for the poor who could not afford them himself. There was no massacre. He allowed Christian worship to continue at the Church of the Holy Sepulchre. The contrast with 1099 — when the First Crusade massacred the population — was noted by everyone. Jerusalem had fallen. Europe's response: the Third Crusade.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Saladin's entry into Jerusalem — organized, controlled, deliberate. No massacre. Christian pilgrims allowed to leave peacefully. The Church of the Holy Sepulchre: Christian worshippers still inside, Saladin's guarantee of safety given. The contrast with 1099 visible in the peace of the city. The Crusader banners coming down. The news spreading west. 16:9.

scenes.title: Richard and Saladin
scenes.narration: The Third Crusade sent the most famous kings in Europe: Richard I of England — the Lionheart. Philip II of France. Frederick Barbarossa of the Holy Roman Empire, who drowned crossing a river. Richard and Saladin fought for two years, trading victories, exchanging gifts and messages of mutual respect. Richard won the coast. He could not take Jerusalem. He left in 1192 having recovered the coast but not the city. A stalemate between the two greatest commanders of the age.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. The Third Crusade — Richard I in full armor, massive, red-cross surcoat. Saladin on horseback. They never met in person but sent gifts and messages: shown as a parallel — Saladin sending fruit and ice to a feverish Richard; Richard gifting Saladin's nephew a knighthood. Two great enemies who respected each other. The coast recovered, Jerusalem unreached. 16:9.

scenes.title: Two Hundred Years of Trying
scenes.narration: After the Third Crusade, seven more Crusades would try to recover Jerusalem. The Fourth Crusade sacked Constantinople — a Christian city — by accident. The Fifth failed in Egypt. The Sixth, led by an excommunicated Holy Roman Emperor, actually negotiated a treaty that returned Jerusalem — for fifteen years, before it was lost again. The Seventh and Eighth both failed in North Africa. The Crusader states shrank, city by city, castle by castle.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. A timeline of the Crusades — each one shown arriving and failing, the Crusader territory on the map shrinking with each decade. The Fourth Crusade sacking Constantinople: Crusaders attacking fellow Christians. The brief return of Jerusalem under treaty: fifteen years, then lost again. The map of Outremer getting smaller and smaller. 16:9.

scenes.title: Acre — The Last City
scenes.narration: 1291. Acre was the last major Crusader city. The Mamluk Sultan al-Ashraf Khalil brought one hundred thousand soldiers and a hundred siege engines. The garrison: fourteen thousand defenders. The walls held for six weeks. On May 18, 1291, the walls were breached. The last Crusaders retreated to a tower on the sea. As the Mamluks undermined its foundations, the tower collapsed, killing everyone inside — besieged and besiegers both. The last Crusader foothold in the Holy Land ended in a collapsing tower.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Acre under siege — the last Crusader city, its harbor full of ships, the walls holding for six weeks against overwhelming force. The breach. The retreat to the sea tower. The Mamluk sappers undermining the foundation. The tower collapsing — Crusaders and Mamluks dying together in the rubble. The harbor, empty. No more Outremer. 16:9.

scenes.title: What They Left
scenes.narration: The Crusader kingdoms lasted about as long as the United States has existed today. They left extraordinary castles — Krak des Chevaliers still stands in Syria, considered the finest medieval castle ever built. They left trade routes, agricultural techniques, and cultural exchange. They left a complicated legacy in the Middle East that has not fully resolved. And they left the proof that a small garrison can hold a city for a very long time if the walls are good enough and the water holds.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Krak des Chevaliers today — the enormous Crusader castle still standing in Syria, magnificent and intact. Other Crusader ruins: castle walls, church foundations, harbor structures. The legacy visible in stone. And the complexity: a landscape that remembers two hundred years of occupation in ways both architectural and historical. 16:9.

scenes.title: Two Hundred Years
scenes.narration: They built kingdoms in a place surrounded by enemies, three thousand miles from home, with no reliable resupply. They held Jerusalem for eighty-eight years. When they lost it, they spent two hundred more years trying to get it back. Eight Crusades. It never worked again. The first time, fueled by a continent's religious fervor, they'd done the impossible. The second time, they couldn't replicate it. Some victories are unrepeatable. Subscribe to Gods & Glory.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Final card — Jerusalem at sunrise, as it stands today. The walls of the old city. The Dome of the Rock. The Church of the Holy Sepulchre. The city three Crusades fought over, the city that broke an era. Still there. 16:9.

viral_hook: The Crusaders marched four thousand miles in three years and took Jerusalem. They held it for eighty-eight years. Then they spent two hundred years trying to get it back and never did.
highlight_note: Scene 4 — Hattin. July heat, no water, fire on the grass, the True Cross falling. The end of Crusader military dominance in one afternoon.
youtube_title: The Crusader Kingdoms: 200 Years in the Holy Land | Gods & Glory
lesson: Holding a place is different from taking it. The Crusaders took Jerusalem in a moment of impossible fervor. Holding it for two hundred years required everything they had, and ultimately more than they could sustain.
